43	
44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Check Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.

14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

72

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.

82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures

219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement

224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings

226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement

434	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
441	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
443	  
444	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
445	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
446	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
447	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
448	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
449	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
450	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
451	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
452	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
453	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
455	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
456	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
457	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
458	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
459	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
460	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
461	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
462	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
463	  \[
464	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
465	  \]
466	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
467	\end{lemma}
468	
469	\begin{proof}
470	  
471	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
472	  exact sequence
473	  \[
474	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
475	  \]
476	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
477	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
478	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
479	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
480	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
481	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
482	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
483	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
484	  \[
485	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
486	  \]
487	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
488	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
489	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
490	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
491	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
492	  \begin{multline*}
493	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
494	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
495	  \end{multline*}
496	  Two ingredients close the proof:
497	
498	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
499	  hypothesis: by REF,
500	  \[
501	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
502	  \]
503	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
504	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
505	
506	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
507	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
508	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
509	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
510	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
511	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
512	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
513	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
514	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
515	
516	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
517	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
518	  \[
519	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
520	  \]
521	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
522	
523	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
524	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
525	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
526	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
527	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
528	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
529	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
530	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
531	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
532	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
533	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
534	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
535	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
536	  complex degenerates).
537	\end{proof}

830	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
831	  over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P\in C\) be a
832	  closed point with coheight witness, let
833	  \(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) be a global section satisfying
834	  \(s\notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) (with \(s_1\) the distinguished constant
835	  section, iter-195 setup line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}), and set
836	  \(f:=\iota(s)\) with \(h_f\colon f\ne 0\) supplied by
837	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}. Then
838	  the principal Weil divisor of \(f\) is non-zero:
839	  \[
840	    \texttt{Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}\,(X:=C.\mathrm{left})\,f\,h_f

846	\begin{proof}
847	  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
848	  Argue by the contrapositive. Suppose for contradiction that
849	  \(\texttt{principal}\,f\,h_f = 0\). The principal divisor is
850	  \(\sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot [Q]\), summed over prime divisors
851	  \(Q\in C.\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}\); the vanishing of the principal
852	  divisor is therefore equivalent to \(\ord_Q(f)=0\) for every prime
853	  divisor \(Q\) of \(C\). Hence \(f\) has no zeros and no poles on
854	  \(C\), and the inclusion
855	  \(\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\) places \(f\) inside
856	  the subsheaf of regular functions on \(C\), i.e.\
857	  \(f\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\). On a complete geometrically
858	  irreducible integral scheme over the algebraically closed field
859	  \(\bar k\) (in particular: smooth proper integral curve), the
860	  ring of global sections of the structure sheaf is exactly the base
861	  field,
862	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks
863	  Project tag \texttt{01XU}); the non-vanishing \(f\ne 0\) plus the
864	  fact that all elements of \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}(\top)\) coincide
865	  with \(\bar k^{\times}\) (the units refinement, Stacks Project tag
866	  \texttt{02P0}) places \(f\in \bar k^{\times}\subset K(C)\).
867	
868	  We now derive a contradiction from the hypothesis
869	  \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Set \(c:=f\in\bar k\), so
870	  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot 1 = 0\) in \(K(C)\), i.e.\
871	  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot \iota(s_1) = 0\) (since \(\iota(s_1)=1\), the
872	  defining equation \texttt{hs1 : toFunctionField s\(_1\) = 1} at
873	  line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}). By the \(\bar k\)-linearity of
874	  \(\iota\) (witnesses \texttt{htF\_smul} at line~1369 and
875	  \texttt{htF\_add} at line~1378 of the iter-195 helper body),
876	  \(\iota(s - c\cdot s_1) = 0\). By
877	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}, the
878	  map \(\iota\) is injective, so \(s - c\cdot s_1 = 0\), i.e.\
879	  \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting the iter-195
880	  hypothesis \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Hence \(\texttt{principal}\,
881	  f\,h_f \ne 0\), as required.

910	\begin{corollary}

923	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
924	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
925	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
926	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
927	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a

930	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
931	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
932	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
933	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
934	  \begin{itemize}

936	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
937	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
938	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
939	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
940	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
941	  \end{itemize}
942	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
943	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
944	\end{corollary}

112	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
113	
114	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
115	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
116	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
117	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
118	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
119	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
120	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/

124	/-! ### Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier (iter-183 Lane A scaffold)
125	
126	The substantive iter-183 contribution: a concrete per-open `Set`-valued
127	carrier of the line bundle `𝒪_C(P)`, realised directly as Hartshorne's
128	subsheaf of the function-field constant sheaf `K_C` (Hartshorne II §6
129	p. 144; analogist `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` Decision 3
130	`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
131	
132	A section of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U` is a rational function `f ∈ K(C)`
133	satisfying the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
134	`Q ≠ P` with `Q.point ∈ U` (regularity on the complement of `P` inside
135	`U`) and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
136	The construction is independent of the choice of uniformiser at `P`:
137	any two uniformisers differ by a unit, so the order-`≥ −1` condition at
138	`P` is intrinsic.
139	
140	iter-184+ will upgrade `carrierSet` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the
141	closure proofs (zero / addition / kbar-scalar), bundle the result as a
142	presheaf functor (identity-on-`K(C)` restrictions, monotone in `U` via
143	`carrierSet_mono`), and discharge the sheaf property via gluing-by-
144	stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at each prime divisor). -/
145	
146	/-- **Carrier set** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U : (Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`: the
147	set of rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` satisfying the order conditions
148	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q` with `Q.point ∈ U.unop`,
149	`Q.point ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U.unop`.
150	
151	iter-183 Lane A landed this as a concrete substantive `Set` definition;
152	no sorry. -/
153	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet
154	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
156	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
157	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
158	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
159	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
160	    Set C.left.functionField := by
161	  let Phat : C.left.PrimeDivisor := ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
162	  haveI := Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
163	    (X := C.left) Phat
164	  exact { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 → Q.point ≠ P →
165	              0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
166	              (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Phat f) }
167	
168	/-- **Monotonicity of `carrierSet` in `U`**: when `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` (i.e.
169	the open `V` is contained in the open `U`), the carrier on `U` is
170	INCLUDED in the carrier on `V` (the order conditions over the smaller
171	open `V` involve fewer prime divisors, hence are easier to satisfy).
172	
173	This is the substantive monotonicity that drives the (identity-on-`K(C)`)
174	restriction map of the would-be `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf` functor:
175	in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`, an arrow `U ⟶ V` corresponds to `V.unop ⊆ U.unop`,
176	and the restriction map `carrierSet U → carrierSet V` is the inclusion
177	delivered by this lemma.

191	  intro f hf

202	  (Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mpr
